title: Justice Is Blind

jokes.text: Barry reveals he hasn't read the plea bargain he just praised and urged the family to take
jokes.setup: Barry confidently tells the family to take the plea because they'll never get an offer this good — building expectation he knows its contents
jokes.punchline: "Well, I didn't read it. I just got it yesterday."
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured comedic reveal. The setup is airtight — Barry's confident salesmanship makes the audience expect competence, so the cold admission of total incompetence lands as a sharp character joke. The economy is excellent; one sentence destroys the entire preceding argument. Strong character integration because Barry's buffoonery as a lawyer is a running gag across the series.

jokes.text: Barry's non-sequitur announcement at a family legal strategy meeting: "F.Y.I., I'm trying to get back into the dating world."
jokes.setup: The family is in crisis mode discussing a criminal plea deal
jokes.punchline: Barry interrupts with completely irrelevant personal news about his dating life
jokes.explanation: The pure absurdity of the timing — a lawyer treating a criminal case discussion like a mixer — is the joke. Economical and character-specific. The tonal collision between crisis and casual personal disclosure is well-executed.

jokes.text: Lucille's response to seeing a woman near the courthouse: "Hey, you're not one of those silly men that's dressed like a woman, are you?"
jokes.setup: Lucille encounters a woman — presumably Maggie or another courthouse woman
jokes.punchline: Lucille's casual, condescending inquiry about cross-dressing, delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: The joke is almost entirely carried by Lucille's character — her unselfconscious rudeness is her defining trait. The line is funny because of how perfectly it encapsulates her blunt, classist worldview. Slightly below average originality since Lucille rudeness jokes are well-trodden territory.

jokes.text: "I don't understand how a person can put their sex life in front of their work." / "He's a man, Michael. He needs a woman."
jokes.setup: Michael expresses genuine frustration that Barry is prioritizing dating over the family's legal crisis
jokes.punchline: Lucille dismisses Michael's concern entirely with a gender-essentialist non-argument, simultaneously revealing her low standards for men
jokes.explanation: Lucille's dismissal is perfectly in character and the contrast with Michael's earnestness works well. Functional character comedy but not particularly inventive.

jokes.text: George Sr. misinterprets Cindi saying "I'm a mole" as referring to her teeth and launches into dental advice
jokes.setup: Cindi has been building to a serious confession — "I'm a mole" — which the audience understands means government informant
jokes.punchline: George Sr. thinks she's talking about her teeth and launches into unsolicited, oddly specific dental advice: "You could grind off about... 30%, maybe more. Yeah, I wouldn't miss it."
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliantly layered misdirection. The audience knows 'mole' means informant, George Sr. doesn't, and his pivot to dental commentary is both absurd and specific enough ("30%, maybe more") to be genuinely funny on its own. The phrase "I wouldn't miss it" about someone else's teeth is darkly perfect. The structure is near-flawless: setup with dramatic weight, punchline that deflates it completely, then a second beat that extends the joke further.

jokes.text: George Sr. wants to fight the case because of Cindi's secret encouragement — "I want to fight this thing. I can beat it. I know it for a fact." — mirroring her exact words
jokes.setup: Cindi has told George Sr. she knows "for a fact" he can beat the case (because she's the informant)
jokes.punchline: George Sr. repeats her exact words verbatim to his family, not knowing the person feeding his confidence is the government agent building the case against him
jokes.explanation: A layered dramatic irony joke — the audience knows something George Sr. doesn't. Works well on rewatch when the full irony of Cindi's position is clear. Solid construction but the punchline lives mostly in the dramatic irony rather than a specific comedic line.

jokes.text: "Is that a shot at me? 'Cause that makes me want to read it less." — Barry, about reading the plea
jokes.setup: The family points out they don't even know what the plea says yet
jokes.punchline: Barry interprets the logical observation as a personal attack and responds by threatening to read it even less
jokes.explanation: Barry's defensive petulance about being called out is both perfectly in character and absurdly childish — a grown attorney threatening to do even less of his job as retaliation. The line is highly quotable and economical. "Makes me want to read it less" is a delightfully specific form of professional negligence.

jokes.text: Michael's reveal that he was in a drama club production of 'The Trial of Captain Hook' to satisfy an English requirement — implying he played a lawyer in 10th grade
jokes.setup: Lucille mocks Michael for thinking he's a lawyer; narrator confirms he "appeared in the drama club's production of an original play, The Trial of Captain Hook"
jokes.punchline: The absurd specificity of the play title — a pirate on trial — and the framing of this as the source of Michael's legal confidence
jokes.explanation: "The Trial of Captain Hook" is exactly absurd enough to be funny. The narrator's deadpan delivery of the title does the heavy lifting. Strong rewatch value because Michael later gives 'Chareth Cutestory, pirate lawyer' as his fake name — this is retroactive foreshadowing of that gag.

jokes.text: "I don't remember that." / "Well, you left during my solo."
jokes.setup: Michael references the Captain Hook play; Lindsay doesn't remember it
jokes.punchline: "Well, you left during my solo" — implying Michael had a musical solo in a legal drama about a pirate, and that Lindsay left mid-show
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical. 'You left during my solo' adds two new absurd layers — the pirate legal drama had musical numbers, and Michael had a solo — in just five words. Also characterizes sibling resentment efficiently. One of the most compressed bits in the episode.

jokes.text: "And I'm surprised my twin sister can remember that, considering she was repeating the ninth grade at the time."
jokes.setup: Lindsay doesn't remember Michael's play
jokes.punchline: Michael reveals Lindsay was held back in 9th grade — a dig wrapped in fake surprise
jokes.explanation: A functional sibling insult joke. The 'twin sister repeating 9th grade' detail is funny but the construction is fairly standard. Gets some extra mileage from Lucille's follow-up.

jokes.text: Tobias's tutoring pitch to the tutor: "I will pack your sweet pink mouth with so much ice cream, you'll be the envy of every Jerry and Jane on the block."
jokes.setup: Tobias and Lindsay are trying to hire a tutor for Maeby but have no money
jokes.punchline: Tobias makes the most sensually bizarre ice cream compensation offer imaginable, with the added detail of "Jerry and Jane" as generic stand-ins — and the tutor points out they're the only house on the block
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. Tobias's unintentional sexual double entendres are a series hallmark, and 'pack your sweet pink mouth with so much ice cream' is perfectly calibrated — exactly suggestive enough to be uncomfortable without being explicit. The 'Jerry and Jane' specificity is a wonderful absurdist flourish. Then the tutor's deadpan 'We're the only house on the block' deflates the entire grandiose offer. This is exceptional character comedy because only Tobias could deliver this line in total innocence.

jokes.text: "We're the only house on the block." — The tutor's flat correction of Tobias's offer
jokes.setup: Tobias promises the tutor will be "the envy of every Jerry and Jane on the block" if she tutors Maeby
jokes.punchline: The tutor points out there are no neighbors to be envied by
jokes.explanation: A perfect straight-man line. The economy is near-perfect — six words destroy Tobias's entire pitch. Works as its own joke but also enhances the previous one.

jokes.text: Gob: 'If you'd slept with more than four women, you'd know that.' / Michael: 'I have, Gob.' / Gob: 'More than five?' / [beat] / 'Let's say five.'
jokes.setup: Gob lectures Michael about one-night stands and implies Michael doesn't have enough experience
jokes.punchline: Gob's numbers escalate upward in disbelief, revealing Michael's actual number is embarrassingly low; the resolution 'Let's say five' is a mercy correction
jokes.explanation: The escalating number game is well-structured: Gob guesses low, Michael says he has more, Gob goes higher, Michael says he has more, Gob says 'more than five?' — the pause confirms he doesn't — and 'Let's say five' is a generous rounding-up that doubles as a joke about how small the real number must be. Perfect Gob/Michael dynamic: Gob's casual promiscuity vs. Michael's embarrassing restraint.

jokes.text: Gob claims Michael has slept with fewer than five women; Michael neither confirms nor denies, and Gob says "Let's say 'five'."
jokes.setup: Gob tells Michael he needs more sexual experience; when pressed on whether Michael has been with more than five women, the conversation stalls
jokes.punchline: "Let's say 'five'" — Gob charitably rounding up for Michael's benefit, implying the real number is lower
jokes.explanation: Gob's performative magnanimity in saying 'let's say five' is perfectly in character — he's both insulting Michael and being fake-generous about it. The rhythm of the exchange is good and the line is quotable.

jokes.text: "These are lawyers... that's Latin for 'liar.'"
jokes.setup: Michael says he's not a one-night stand kind of guy because he doesn't like lying to women; Gob says the women in the bar are lawyers
jokes.punchline: "That's Latin for 'liar'" — a fake etymology that happens to be thematically perfect
jokes.explanation: This is a standout line. The fake-pedantic delivery of false Latin etymology to make a point about dishonesty is deeply funny and very Gob-specific. It's also structurally perfect — Michael's objection about lying is immediately undermined by reframing the women as professional liars. Highly quotable, economical, and unique to Gob's brand of confident pseudo-intelligence.

jokes.text: Maggie Lizer introduces herself: "I'm Maggie Lizer, as in 'Maggie lies her ass off.'" Then: "One person laughed at that once, and I don't know why I keep trying it."
jokes.setup: Maggie introduces herself with a pun on her own name
jokes.punchline: She immediately undercuts the joke by noting its terrible track record, then keeps doing it anyway
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional meta-comedy. The pun on 'Lizer/liar' is set up as a groan-worthy name joke — but then Maggie immediately acknowledges it as such, producing a second, stronger laugh from the self-awareness. It then foreshadows the show's central irony: Maggie herself is lying about being blind, her name is a meta-joke about deception, and she works as a prosecutor. Three layers of meaning in two sentences. The structure is near-perfect: setup, failed punchline, meta-commentary on the failure. One of the episode's highlights.

jokes.text: "Submarine chaser." — Maggie's response to learning Michael does maritime law
jokes.setup: Michael says he does maritime law
jokes.punchline: Maggie responds with "Submarine chaser" — a legal term (ambulance chaser, but for submarines) delivered perfectly deadpan
jokes.explanation: A two-word joke that perfectly combines wordplay with character — Maggie is a sharp, witty prosecutor who immediately has fun with Michael's absurd fake specialty. 'Submarine chaser' as a riff on 'ambulance chaser' is clever and the delivery (deadpan, instant) is what makes it work. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Michael gives his fake name: 'Chareth Cutestory' — Maggie says 'Cutestory?' / 'Yes. Chareth Cutestory.' / 'That's an interesting name.' / 'Is it?'
jokes.setup: Michael needs a fake last name and clearly panics
jokes.punchline: He combines 'cute story' into a surname; Maggie accepts it as 'interesting' without suspicion; Michael's 'Is it?' plays confused innocence
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable moments. 'Chareth Cutestory' is a perfect absurdist improvised name — it sounds almost plausible, which is the joke. 'Is it?' as Michael's response to 'that's an interesting name' is the ideal deadpan; he's committing fully to the bit. The name becomes a recurring callback throughout the episode and beyond, earning its high craft score through originality, structure, and total quotability.

jokes.text: Maeby's school test where she wrote 'S. Fünke' — tutor explains that 'S' means single, not her name initial
jokes.setup: The tutor reviews Maeby's failed test and notes she got her own name wrong — it says 'S. Fünke'
jokes.punchline: The 'S' wasn't her initial — it was the 'single/married' marital status field, which Maeby filled with her name
jokes.explanation: A nicely observed joke about a specific kind of standardized test confusion. Works well as character comedy for Maeby — she's not dumb, she's just indifferent. The tutor's patient explanation adds to the humor.

jokes.text: Maeby pays the tutor $120 (six $20s) claiming it's $200 — and the tutor notices but lets it go
jokes.setup: Maeby offers the tutor $200 for doing her homework; Tobias had promised an ice cream arrangement
jokes.punchline: Maeby pays $120, calls it $200 — the tutor counts it, realizes, but pockets it anyway
jokes.explanation: Both characters' corruption is revealed simultaneously — Maeby is casually dishonest about the amount, the tutor knowingly accepts being shortchanged anyway. The economy of the exchange ("That's right." / "Right.") is very funny.

jokes.text: Tutor asks Maeby 'Are you getting paid for this?' / Maeby: 'There was talk of ice cream, but not exactly on my terms.'
jokes.setup: The tutor notes she's supposed to be tutoring Maeby
jokes.punchline: Maeby references the Tobias ice cream offer — 'not exactly on my terms' — showing Maeby also found it absurd
jokes.explanation: A quick callback to Tobias's ice cream offer that confirms both Maeby's awareness of how weird her family is and that the tutor situation is entirely chaotic. 'Not exactly on my terms' is a great deadpan for a teenager.

jokes.text: Narrator: "The next morning, Michael was wondering how to end his first one-night stand."
jokes.setup: Michael claimed he's not a one-night stand kind of guy; after drinking with Maggie he gave her a ride home
jokes.punchline: The narrator dryly confirms Michael is indeed trying to execute a one-night stand exit strategy
jokes.explanation: The narrator functioning as ironic counterpoint to Michael's self-image is a classic AD structure. Competently executed, though not the sharpest instance of this device.

jokes.text: "Is he as handsome as he smells?" — Maggie asking Justice the dog about Michael
jokes.setup: Maggie introduces Michael to her dog Justice
jokes.punchline: Maggie asks the dog whether Michael is as handsome as he smells — both funny because she's consulting a dog and because it reveals she can't see Michael
jokes.explanation: A good visual/character joke. On first watch it's charming; on rewatch, knowing Maggie isn't blind, it's a deliberately performed moment. Both readings are funny, though for different reasons.

jokes.text: "Well, it's tough with your hands in the way." — Michael's comment when asked to show his smile
jokes.setup: Maggie asks to see Michael's smile while reaching toward his face
jokes.punchline: Michael notes his smile is obscured by her hands — a gentle, awkward observation
jokes.explanation: A mild, functional line. The awkwardness of the moment carries more than the joke itself. Below average for this episode.

jokes.text: Michael flashes back to Maggie saying "I have an irreversible case of ocular retinoblastoma" and he responded "Hey, I thought we said no more law talk"
jokes.setup: Maggie mentioned a medical condition the night before; drunk Michael assumed all unfamiliar terminology was legal jargon
jokes.punchline: He interrupted her disclosure of blindness to say "no more law talk" — he thought her eye condition was a case name
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. It works on multiple levels: (1) the retroactive revelation reframes the previous night entirely, (2) Michael's drunken logic is perfectly consistent with his established anxiety about 'no law talk,' (3) the medical terminology sounds legal enough to sell the misunderstanding. The structure of delivering it as a flashback at the moment Michael 'recalls' it is excellent timing. Also foreshadows the whole 'Justice Is Blind' theme.

jokes.text: Flashback exchange: "I really can't see anybody right now." / "Oh, well, I can't see anybody ever. I'm blind." / "I'm wasted."
jokes.setup: In the flashback, both characters misunderstand each other perfectly — Michael means he can't date anyone, Maggie states she's blind
jokes.punchline: "I'm wasted" — Michael's response to learning she's blind, which is simultaneously him explaining his mental state AND reacting to the information
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and one of the great AD jokes overall. Three lines, three meanings, perfect structure. "I really can't see anybody right now" (Michael: dating, Maggie: literally seeing). "I can't see anybody ever" (Maggie clarifying). "I'm wasted" (Michael: 1. explaining his state, 2. his reaction to the news, 3. confirming why he missed the information). The triple meaning of 'wasted' — drunk, in response to hearing she's blind, and as a throwaway non-sequitur — is compressed genius. Comparable in craft to the show's finest moments.

jokes.text: Narrator: "Michael realized that the woman he was attempting to never see again was blind."
jokes.setup: Michael has been trying to execute a one-night stand exit
jokes.punchline: The narrator's phrasing — "attempting to never see again" — takes on a double meaning when the woman is revealed to be literally blind and also will never see him again
jokes.explanation: The narrator's line is perfectly engineered. "Attempting to never see again" is a hilariously clinical way to describe ghosting someone, and it resonates doubly because Maggie literally cannot see him. The narrator in AD often functions as a deadpan joke machine and this is among the sharpest instances in the episode.

jokes.text: Michael's guilt monologue: "Come on. I took a blind woman home with no intention of dating her again? Please!"
jokes.setup: Michael is asking if he got 'one-night stand-itis'; he's clearly lying to himself
jokes.punchline: His indignant denial — "Please!" — is transparently self-serving; the audience knows he only feels guilty because she's blind
jokes.explanation: Works as a character beat for Michael — his self-righteousness masking shallow motivation. The "Please!" delivery is very Jason Bateman. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: George Sr. and Cindi are discovered tweaking nipples through a chain-link fence; Lucille arrives
jokes.setup: George Sr. has been trying to extract legal information from Cindi while in prison; Lucille arrives unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: "It looks like you're tweaking her nipples through a chain-link fence." / "Yep. Yeah, that's it."
jokes.explanation: "Tweaking her nipples through a chain-link fence" is such a specific, vivid image that it's inherently funny. George Sr.'s flat "Yep. Yeah, that's it." — choosing not to explain — is perfect character comedy. The physical absurdity of the visual (inferred from context) combined with the deadpan acceptance earns its high marks.

jokes.text: Lucille calls Cindi "this great redwood of a whore"
jokes.setup: Lucille is furious at George Sr. for the nipple-tweaking incident with Cindi
jokes.punchline: "Great redwood of a whore" — an insult that combines a tree metaphor with profanity in a startlingly specific way
jokes.explanation: "Great redwood of a whore" is a masterclass in Lucille Bluth insult construction. The specificity of 'redwood' — implying tall, thick, and ancient — delivered with aristocratic fury is pitch-perfect. Only Lucille would reach for arboreal metaphor in this moment. One of the most quotable lines in the episode. The character integration is exceptional; no one else on this show could say this.

jokes.text: George Sr.'s defense of Cindi: "She has seen everything I've done. You understand? Everything."
jokes.setup: George Sr. is defending his relationship with Cindi; unbeknownst to him, 'everything she's seen' includes his Caged Wisdom videos AND the government evidence against him
jokes.punchline: "Everything" carries enormous dramatic irony — she has seen everything, including as a government mole building his case
jokes.explanation: A solid dramatic irony beat. The double meaning of 'Everything' works well and gains on rewatch. Not laugh-out-loud funny on first viewing but earns its place in the episode's irony architecture.

jokes.text: Cindi laments being a home-wrecker; George Sr. comforts her, clearly not meaning it
jokes.setup: Cindi feels guilty about George Sr.'s marriage
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s repeated "No, no. No. No, Cindi" — a perfectly calibrated non-comfort from a self-serving man
jokes.explanation: This is more of a character/plot beat than a strong joke. The comedy is mild — George Sr.'s transparently self-interested comfort is a known quantity at this point in the show.

jokes.text: Cindi, after causing the Bluth marriage to explode: 'Oh, God. I'm a home-wrecker. I never wanted to be that person.'
jokes.setup: Cindi is literally a government informant who was sent specifically to investigate the Bluths and seduce George
jokes.punchline: Her genuine anguish about being a 'home-wrecker' ignores the far worse thing she's done (entrapping George as a mole)
jokes.explanation: Ironic because Cindi's moral code draws the line at home-wrecking but not at being a government entrapment agent. Her misplaced guilt is funny because it ignores the larger ethical issue.

jokes.text: Gob's brag: "You could have seen me get some major action from a major blonde who just majored in marine biology, if you know what I mean."
jokes.setup: Gob is recapping his night to Michael
jokes.punchline: The triple "major" construction is absurd, and the marine biology detail makes the sexual implication baffling
jokes.explanation: The triple deployment of 'major' is a Gob-specific verbal tic blown up to absurdity. "if you know what I mean" placed after "marine biology" implies there IS a meaning Michael is supposed to understand — and there isn't. Very Gob. High character integration.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I told you to walk away. I told you to give a fake name.' / Michael: 'I did. Thanks very much. I'm Chareth Cutestory, a pirate lawyer.'
jokes.setup: Gob claims he didn't set Michael up with the blind woman situation
jokes.punchline: 'I'm Chareth Cutestory, a pirate lawyer' — Michael's callback to his fake identity combining the maritime law cover with the absurd surname
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's most quotable line. 'Chareth Cutestory, a pirate lawyer' combines two earlier jokes (the fake name and 'lawyers of the sea') into a callback that's funnier than either original joke. The addition of 'pirate lawyer' as Michael's summary of his own cover story is an absurdist escalation. 'It was airtight' as Michael's defense is the cherry on top. This is genuinely great sitcom writing — economical, built from previous material, and more rewarding on rewatch. Deserves its 8+ rating.

jokes.text: Gob: "You've just won the gold medal at the sexual Special Olympics! She can't ever find you again. Don't you see that you're so lucky?"
jokes.setup: Michael feels guilty about having a one-night stand with a blind woman
jokes.punchline: Gob frames the situation as an Olympic achievement — being with someone who can't identify you afterward
jokes.explanation: One of Gob's defining moments. The 'gold medal at the sexual Special Olympics' is exactly the kind of appalling logic Gob would deploy — framing predatory behavior as athletic achievement. It's dark, it's specific, and it's completely consistent with Gob's moral framework. The phrase 'Don't you see' to a person discussing a blind woman adds another layer. High quotability and exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: Michael cuts off Gob's advice with "God! How do you not s—" then catches himself
jokes.setup: Michael is about to say "How do you not see" to Gob — which would be ironic given the discussion is about a blind woman
jokes.punchline: Michael catches himself mid-word, realizing the irony; the audience catches it too
jokes.explanation: A perfectly observed character beat — Michael's verbal stumble is both a real reaction and a self-correcting joke. The interrupted 'see' is seamlessly woven into the dialogue. Strong structural use of the episode's blindness theme.

jokes.text: Gob reveals he got married on a dare the previous night — to a woman who dared him to do things
jokes.setup: Michael criticizes Gob for sleeping with someone he'll never see again
jokes.punchline: "She was a darer. She's one of those girls who just dares you to do things." / "You married her?" / "I needed a dare!"
jokes.explanation: "I needed a dare!" might be the most Gob sentence ever uttered. The escalation from 'I slept with someone' to 'I married a stranger because she dared me' is perfectly constructed, and 'I needed a dare' as the explanation is both absurd and psychologically revealing about Gob's need for external validation. Character integration is near-perfect — this could only be Gob. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Gob reveals he accidentally got married: 'She knows that I'm Gob Bluth, and we got married, and I just...' Michael: 'What?' Gob: 'Well, she was a darer.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Gob about his own woman from the courthouse bar.
jokes.punchline: Gob broke his own rules (real name, promises) — he gave his real name and got married because she was 'a darer' — someone who dares you to do things.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob story structure. He just finished lecturing Michael on 'no real names, no promises' — and immediately revealed he violated both to the maximum possible degree. 'She was a darer' as the explanation is both absurd and entirely believable for Gob, whose impulse control is nonexistent. 'I needed a dare' as elaboration is the perfect puncture.

jokes.text: Narrator explains that despite building dares, "the one thing they never dared each other to do was consummate the marriage"
jokes.setup: Gob and his new wife have been escalating dares all night
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals they did everything except actually consummate the marriage — the one relevant act — making the marriage legally annullable
jokes.explanation: The narrator delivers this as pure deadpan exposition but it's structurally a great comedy reveal — the one dare they forgot is the one that matters most. Also sets up the later annulment joke perfectly.

jokes.text: "Do you ever listen to what I say?" / "I'm sorry I don't memorize every word that comes out of your mouth. Sometimes I just like to think... think my thoughts."
jokes.setup: Gob is exasperated that his new wife didn't know about his seal business
jokes.punchline: His wife defends her right to "think her thoughts" — a grandiose defense of not listening that sounds philosophical but is just stubbornness
jokes.explanation: "Sometimes I just like to think... think my thoughts" is a weirdly funny line — the pause before 'think my thoughts' makes it sound like she's discovering this philosophy in real time. Good economy.

jokes.text: "It was hot." — Narrator's one-line assessment of Gob and his wife's argument
jokes.setup: Gob and his new wife have just had a bickering argument about seals
jokes.punchline: The narrator provides an editorial opinion — "It was hot" — characterizing their domestic argument as romantic tension
jokes.explanation: Three words, maximum impact. The narrator stepping out of his documentary role to editorialize about a fight being 'hot' is both absurd and perfectly timed. Economy is flawless. The narrator in AD occasionally breaks frame in ways that reveal he has opinions, and this is among the cleanest examples.

jokes.text: Gob: "Believe me, we didn't do any sleeping. I had sex last night." Narrator: "But he really didn't."
jokes.setup: Michael says "boy, the lengths you'll go to sleep with a woman"; Gob corrects the euphemism
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately contradicts Gob with "But he really didn't" — a flat, omniscient denial of Gob's bravado
jokes.explanation: The narrator undercutting Gob's machismo with a flat "But he really didn't" is one of the show's most enjoyable structural tricks. Gob's performative bravado is instantly deflated by omniscient narration. The repetition of this device ("But he really didn't" is repeated again after Gob's later brag) makes it a mini-running gag within the episode. Economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: Maggie: "I thought I smelled you coming." / Michael: "Mm. Yeah, here I am."
jokes.setup: Michael arrives for his date; Maggie, who is blind, identifies him by smell
jokes.punchline: "Mm. Yeah, here I am." — Michael's flat, slightly uncomfortable confirmation that he has been smelled
jokes.explanation: Light character comedy. Michael's flat response to being identified by scent is mildly funny — there's no good answer to 'I smelled you coming.' Rewatch value because Maggie actually can see him, making 'I smelled you' a performance.

jokes.text: "You don't have any evidence of handsome." — Michael's response to Maggie calling him handsome
jokes.setup: Maggie says she thinks Michael is handsome; Michael notes she's blind
jokes.punchline: "You don't have any evidence of handsome" — using the language of the courtroom (evidence) to gently point out she can't actually see him
jokes.explanation: Elegant wordplay using legal terminology ('evidence') in a personal context — and it works doubly because Maggie IS a lawyer. 'No evidence of handsome' is highly quotable and lands beautifully. Rewatch value: Maggie can actually see him, so her "Well, I can feel handsome" is also a performed response.

jokes.text: "So you didn't get a case of 'I just had a one-night stand with a blind girl'-itus?"
jokes.setup: Maggie is surprised Michael came back for a second date
jokes.punchline: She coins the syndrome "-itus" — treating guilty post-one-night-stand feelings as a diagnosable medical condition with an absurdly specific name
jokes.explanation: The medical suffix '-itis' applied to a hyper-specific situation is a very AD construction. The absurdly long condition name is also self-aware — Maggie is sharper than she initially appeared, and her casual use of the term suggests this is something she's dealt with before. Rewatch bonus: she's not blind, so the 'blind girl' framing is itself a performance.

jokes.text: Maggie: 'So you didn't get a case of I-just-had-a-one-night-stand-with-a-blind-girl-itus?'
jokes.setup: Maggie expected Michael to ghost her after the one-night stand.
jokes.punchline: She names the condition he presumably has with a medical-ish portmanteau — 'blind-girl-itus' — with complete self-awareness.
jokes.explanation: A confident, self-aware joke from Maggie that establishes her as sharp and not a victim. The clinical suffix '-itus' applied to a social situation is a nice bit of wordplay, and her casual naming of exactly what Michael is experiencing is both funny and character-defining.

jokes.text: Michael offers to be Maggie's "Seeing Eye dog" on their walk
jokes.setup: Michael and Maggie are going for a walk; Justice the seeing-eye dog is present
jokes.punchline: Michael volunteers to be Maggie's 'Seeing Eye dog' — replacing the dog named Justice with himself, while trying to avoid justice (the concept) in his own life
jokes.explanation: A light thematic joke — Michael literally replacing the dog named Justice while evading justice figuratively. Works better as a rewatch observation than a first-watch laugh.

jokes.text: Michael starts narrating the park to Maggie: 'There's an elderly couple walking hand in hand... Three years is what I give them, and then one of them dies...'
jokes.setup: Maggie asks Michael to describe the beautiful park to her. He begins with an elderly couple.
jokes.punchline: He immediately pivots to predicting their imminent deaths — 'three years is what I give them, and then one of them dies and the other one is not too far behind.' He's been reading the plea, not looking at the park.
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully structured bit. The setup is warmly romantic — describe the beautiful park — and Michael starts with an apparently sweet image (elderly couple, hand in hand). The pivot to 'three years is what I give them' as he reads the plea's 'no less than three' is both dark and structurally brilliant. It reveals he's reading instead of watching, makes a morbid joke, and shows his mind is somewhere else. The logic of 'one of them dies and the other one is not too far behind' delivered as park narration is absurdist and dark in the best way.

jokes.text: Michael describes the park to Maggie while secretly reading the plea document — his descriptions of the 'elderly couple' are actually him reading the plea
jokes.setup: Maggie asks Michael to describe the park to her; Michael needs to read the plea
jokes.punchline: "Already served, not to exceed six years but no less than three... Uh, three years is what I give them... and then one of them dies and the other one is not too far behind." — Michael blends the plea language with his fictional elderly couple description
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's finest sustained joke constructions. Michael's description of the 'elderly couple' transitions seamlessly into the plea's legal language — "not to exceed six years but no less than three" becomes a prediction of how long the couple has to live. "And then one of them dies and the other one is not too far behind" is darkly funny as both a plea reading and an observation about elderly couples. Beautifully layered, callbacks the plea running gag, and earns its high score by the specificity of the crossover language.

jokes.text: Maggie: "I can smell right through you, Chareth. You're reading."
jokes.setup: Michael has been reading the plea while pretending to describe the park
jokes.punchline: Maggie catches him by smell — "I can smell right through you" — which also means she sees through his deception, making 'smell' a metaphor for perception that a blind person would use
jokes.explanation: 'I can smell right through you' is a sharply written line — it uses sensory substitution (smell for sight) to express seeing through deception. Rewatch bonus: Maggie actually can see him reading, making 'smell' a deliberate blind performance.

jokes.text: Maggie: 'I can smell right through you, Chareth. You're reading.' / Michael: 'Leash your dog, you...' [stops]
jokes.setup: Maggie catches Michael reading the plea instead of describing the park, by smell
jokes.punchline: Michael's angry retort about leashing the dog is cut off — he realizes Maggie IS the seeing-eye, and criticizing the dog is both insulting and gives away his frustration
jokes.explanation: The 'I can smell right through you' is another blindness tell (she detects deceit by smell). Michael's cut-off insult is a reaction beat. Both work together to sustain the misdirection and Michael's cringe-worthy social fumbling.

jokes.text: Warden Gentles reveals he enjoyed Caged Wisdom and specifically mentions the 'blooper bonus footage' including George Sr. nearly strangling himself with his own prayer shawl
jokes.setup: The Warden seems to have had a genuine spiritual experience watching Caged Wisdom
jokes.punchline: The source of his inspiration was the blooper reel — specifically George Sr. accidentally choking himself on his prayer shawl
jokes.explanation: The detail of "A self-choking for the ages" as a line from a spiritual video series blooper reel is perfectly absurd. The Warden's deadpan reverence for it adds to the joke.

jokes.text: George Sr. discovers Cindi's "I know for a fact" was actually "I have faith" — and the key distinction: she misheard his blooper where he said "Faith is a facet" as "Faith is a fact"
jokes.setup: Throughout the episode, Cindi has been saying she knows 'for a fact' George Sr. can beat the case; George Sr. has been reading this as insider knowledge
jokes.punchline: "Faith is not a fact!" / "Oh yes it is. You said so yourself in Caged Wisdom." — she heard the blooper, not the real content; the word George Sr. said was 'facet,' not 'fact'
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional multi-layered payoff. The episode has been building toward the revelation that Cindi's 'I know for a fact' is actually faith-based — but the resolution goes one better: she got the 'fact' from a BLOOPER REEL where George Sr. misspoke. 'Faith is a facet' vs 'Faith is a fact' is the source of everything. The structure across the entire episode — fact/faith thread, Caged Wisdom blooper, Cindi's informant status — all converge here perfectly. "I am going to trial because you don't understand what a blooper reel is? Guard!" is the deserved capper.

jokes.text: George Sr. in the blooper footage: "Faith is a fact. No. Faith is a facet. I almost said faith is a fact."
jokes.setup: Cindi believed George Sr. said "Faith is a fact" in Caged Wisdom
jokes.punchline: The blooper footage shows George Sr. saying "Faith is a fact. No. Faith is a facet. I almost said faith is a fact." — catching himself, explaining his own mistake, and still being misunderstood
jokes.explanation: The blooper footage itself is a joke — George Sr. says the wrong thing, corrects himself, and explains the correction, yet it's the wrong-thing-plus-correction that Cindi heard and believed. The self-correction making things worse is a perfect structural irony. George Sr.'s fury — "I am going to trial because you don't understand what a blooper reel is?" — is a great capper.

jokes.text: Maeby has an 'A' on a test she clearly didn't earn — she's been paying her own tutor and collecting the money
jokes.setup: The tutor was hired to bring Maeby's grades up; George Michael finds Maeby at school with an A
jokes.punchline: Maeby is running a scam — paying the tutor to do her own tests while collecting money from her parents and the tutor
jokes.explanation: Maeby's con is a nice payoff to the tutoring setup. The economy of 'Great job. Take some more money' reveals the scam neatly without over-explanation.

jokes.text: Michael confesses he didn't read the plea: "I definitely definitely read this uh... the first part of this first, uh, section." Long pause. "Uh, no. Did not read it."
jokes.setup: Michael has been asked if he read the plea; he starts his standard responsible-Michael routine
jokes.punchline: The elaborate setup collapses into a flat admission: "Uh, no. Did not read it."
jokes.explanation: The running gag about the unread plea culminates in Michael — the responsible one — also not having read it. The false start and collapse of his excuse is well-performed comedy. "Did not read it" as the final flat admission matches Barry's opener perfectly.

jokes.text: Lucille: "How many times are you going to screw up before the rest of this family sees you for the screwup you are?"
jokes.setup: Michael is being criticized for not reading the plea
jokes.punchline: Lucille delivers a devastating insult at Michael — calling him a screwup — when she has spent the episode ignoring the plea, having a catfight at a prison, and being generally oblivious
jokes.explanation: Standard Lucille projection comedy. The irony is present but not particularly sharpened in this instance. Functional character moment.

jokes.text: Gob can't get an annulment because he didn't consummate the marriage — but insists he did, for his own dignity
jokes.setup: Barry explains annulment requires non-consummation; Gob knows they didn't consummate but can't admit it
jokes.punchline: "Well, I guess I'll just stay married then, because we all know that I closed that deal, right?"
jokes.explanation: Gob choosing to remain trapped in a marriage rather than admit he didn't have sex is hilariously on-brand. 'We all know I closed that deal, right?' is both a callback and a perfect Gob line — seeking external validation for a lie. The callback to 'seal the deal' adds resonance.

jokes.text: Lucille reveals she and George Sr. are divorcing; reassures Michael/Lindsay: "Oh, don't worry, sweetie. No one is fighting over you."
jokes.setup: Lucille announces she's getting divorced; Michael/Lindsay react with concern about what this means for them
jokes.punchline: Lucille's comfort is that neither parent wants custody — "No one is fighting over you" — delivered as if it's reassuring
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional Lucille character comedy. The attempted reassurance — "No one is fighting over you" — is simultaneously the cruelest possible thing to say to a child of divorce and delivered as genuine comfort. The character integration is near-perfect: only Lucille's specific blend of obliviousness and cruelty produces this exact line. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Lucille: "You'd think a man locked up in prison would be able to abstain."
jokes.setup: Lucille is complaining about George Sr.'s relationship with Cindi
jokes.punchline: She frames prison as a logistical opportunity for abstinence, seemingly unaware of how prison actually works
jokes.explanation: Lucille's logic is consistent with her worldview — prison is simply inconvenient, not actually limiting. Functional character comedy.

jokes.text: Lucille: "Your father, with his disgusting tweaking!" / [beat] / "I couldn't breast-feed any of you kids because of that man!"
jokes.setup: Lucille is airing grievances about George Sr.'s nipple-tweaking habit
jokes.punchline: The escalation from current complaint to an ancient, specific resentment about breast-feeding connects the dots in a horrifying and hilarious way
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's standout jokes. The logic is airtight in the worst possible way — if George Sr.'s tweaking ruined Lucille's nipples for breast-feeding, then the Bluth children's upbringing was affected by this specific sexual habit. The callback to the tweaking scene plus the escalation to a decades-old trauma is perfectly constructed. "I couldn't breast-feed any of you kids because of that man!" is both horrifying and hilarious. The Aww/Shh exchange from the children adds the perfect cap.

jokes.text: "They still look fabulous." — George Sr.'s response to Lucille's breast-feeding complaint
jokes.setup: Lucille accuses George Sr. of ruining her ability to breast-feed through nipple tweaking
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s defense is not denial but a compliment about the current state of her breasts
jokes.explanation: "They still look fabulous" is a perfectly timed non-defense — George Sr. doesn't apologize, doesn't deny, just offers an appreciative observation about the outcome. The shamelessness is the joke. It's character-perfect and extremely quotable. The economy (four words) is near-perfect.

jokes.text: George Sr. reveals he slept with Lucille's sister — but frames it as having been a good thing "Got her to stop drinking, didn't it?"
jokes.setup: Lucille says she should have never doubted George Sr. "even when you slept with my sister"
jokes.punchline: George Sr. accepts this framing and justifies the infidelity with its apparent therapeutic side effect: it cured his sister-in-law's drinking
jokes.explanation: The logic is wonderfully twisted — George Sr. treating his affair as a public health intervention is deeply in character. "Got her to stop drinking, didn't it?" delivered with genuine pride is both appalling and hilarious. The casual acceptance of the premise by everyone in the room is the dark topping.

jokes.text: The family collectively decides to take the plea without reading it, persuaded by Lucille saying "It's so nice and thick."
jokes.setup: The plea has been the MacGuffin of the episode — everyone refuses to read it but has opinions about it
jokes.punchline: Lucille's persuasive argument for taking it is that it's "nice and thick" — the family agrees
jokes.explanation: The perfect culmination of the plea running gag. The family's entire legal strategy is based on the document's physical heft. "It's so nice and thick" as legal reasoning is exactly the right absurdist capstone. Everyone agreeing without any additional reasoning is consistent with how the episode has treated the plea throughout.

jokes.text: Nobody will read the plea — even in the courthouse. Lucille: 'It is thick. Why don't we just take it?'
jokes.setup: Michael pleads with the family that they can't accept conditions they don't know. He offers to read it.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's objection to reading the plea is simply that it's thick. She'd rather blindly accept an unknown agreement than read something long.
jokes.explanation: The final beat on the unread plea gag before the courtroom scene. Lucille's reasoning — 'it's thick, just take it' — is simultaneously stupid and perfectly Lucille. The plea's thickness has been noted before; now it becomes the decisive factor in a major legal decision.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals Maggie is the prosecutor — "Yes, Maggie was the most feared prosecutor in all of Orange County."
jokes.setup: The Bluth family notes "We've got Lizer" as the prosecutor — Michael has been dating the woman who is prosecuting his father
jokes.punchline: The narrator's deadpan confirmation of Maggie's fearsome legal reputation, juxtaposed with Michael's shock
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Michael has been sleeping with the prosecutor is an excellent comedic twist. The narrator's matter-of-fact framing — 'most feared prosecutor in all of Orange County' — contrasts perfectly with the chaos of Michael recognizing her.

jokes.text: Narrator explains Maggie wins cases "not because she was a particularly adept lawyer, but because she won the sympathy... of the defendants themselves" — cut to a defendant saying "I was in that room"
jokes.setup: Maggie is established as the most feared prosecutor in Orange County
jokes.punchline: Her secret weapon isn't legal skill — it's that defendants feel so sorry for her as a blind woman that they confess on the stand
jokes.explanation: This is an outstanding character revelation joke. The idea that a blind prosecutor wins through sympathy-induced confessions — and is therefore the MOST FEARED — is a perfectly original comedic concept. The structure is flawless: setup (most feared), subversion (not for her skill), payoff (defendants confess). Cut to a defendant saying 'I was in that room' while she's questioning him is the perfect illustration. Rewatch bonus: knowing Maggie isn't blind makes this even darker and funnier.

jokes.text: Michael panics in court: "You gotta get more time. Get more time. She can smell me. She smells everything."
jokes.setup: Michael recognizes Maggie as the prosecutor; he's trying to hide from a blind woman who identifies people by smell
jokes.punchline: His paranoid terror that a blind person can detect him is played completely straight
jokes.explanation: Michael's terror of a blind woman smelling him in a crowded courtroom is a nice comedic escalation of the 'she smells everything' running thread. On rewatch, even funnier because Maggie can see him perfectly well.

jokes.text: Gob: "That's the blind chick you slept with?" Lindsay: "Michael, you're in bed with the prosecution?" Gob: "It's genius. You can get all the information we need."
jokes.setup: Michael recognizes the prosecutor as Maggie; the family immediately processes it as an opportunity
jokes.punchline: Gob's pivot from judgment to opportunism in one sentence: the family wants Michael to exploit his relationship with the prosecutor
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's "in bed with the prosecution" functions as both literal truth and legal metaphor, which is nicely efficient. Gob's immediate turn to opportunism is in character.

jokes.text: "The solution to all our problems is staring you right in the face and it can't even see you."
jokes.setup: The family is urging Michael to exploit Maggie's blindness and their relationship
jokes.punchline: Gob's summary of the situation — the solution is literally looking at Michael but literally cannot see him — uses 'staring' and 'see' in the context of a blind woman
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines and among the finest in AD's canon of wordplay. 'Staring you right in the face and it can't even see you' works as idiom, as literal description of the situation, and as the episode's thematic statement. It's delivered by Gob, who is usually the least articulate family member — the irony of Gob producing the episode's cleverest line is itself funny. Economy is excellent. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Gob: 'The solution to all our problems is staring you right in the face and it can't even see you.'
jokes.setup: Gob is urging Michael to exploit the fact that Maggie is blind to get legal information
jokes.punchline: 'Staring you right in the face and it can't even see you' — the metaphor inverted on itself with the literal blindness of Maggie
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. 'Staring you right in the face and it can't even see you' is a perfectly constructed inversion of the cliché — the idiom is reversed to literally describe Maggie's blindness. It's grammatically clever (the solution can't see; the problem is the blind woman), highly quotable, and maximally economical. Gob delivering a grammatically brilliant observation he probably doesn't fully understand makes it even better. This easily clears the 8+ threshold: it's original, structurally excellent, and has the kind of elegant wordplay that would make any comedy writer jealous.

jokes.text: George Michael discovers Maeby has been running a fake sick twin scam at school fund-raisers as 'Surely Fünke'
jokes.setup: Maeby told George Michael she was going to a fund-raiser for a sick student; George Michael thought this was out of character
jokes.punchline: "Please welcome... Surely Fünke!" — Maeby is pretending to be a sick twin named Surely to collect charity money
jokes.explanation: The Surely Fünke reveal is one of the episode's best moments and one of AD's great running gag origins. 'Surely' as a sick twin's name is a perfect pun/joke name (Surely = Shirley, the stock sick child name, plus the audacity of using your own surname). The reveal that Maeby has constructed an entire fake identity to bilk her classmates of charity money is perfectly consistent with her character and completely surprising. This joke plants a seed for multiple episodes of callbacks. The 'Somebody get her a cupcake!' tag is icing on a sublime cake.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Yeah, I better get back to the wife. I think I'm going to take a scenic drive down to the City of Industry.'
jokes.setup: Gob has just tried to pressure Michael into exploiting a blind woman
jokes.punchline: Gob's transition to 'I better get back to the wife' then 'scenic drive to the City of Industry' — the City of Industry is a notoriously industrial, non-scenic area; the phrase 'scenic drive' is pure ironic misnomer
jokes.explanation: The 'City of Industry' joke is a geography-specific gag (the City of Industry in LA is anything but scenic). Works for those who know it, falls flat otherwise. Regional comedy.

jokes.text: "Somebody get her a cupcake!" — reaction to Maeby's reveal as Surely Fünke
jokes.setup: Maeby is revealed as the 'sick' student at the fund-raiser
jokes.punchline: Someone in the crowd's response is to call for a cupcake for the sick girl — the most minor form of charity imaginable
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on the Surely reveal. The crowd's response — not medical help, not money, but a cupcake — deflates the high drama of the scam with mundane charity. Economy is nearly perfect.

jokes.text: Maggie gives Michael the government's entire case summary against the Bluth family — then asks "Do we have a case?" revealing she trusts a man she met in a bar to evaluate the evidence against his own family
jokes.setup: Michael is at Maggie's ostensibly to come clean; Maggie trusts him because he's been a supportive presence
jokes.punchline: She hands him the complete government case file and asks him — the suspect's son and family fixer — whether she has a case
jokes.explanation: The structural irony here is rich — the entire episode has been about Michael's guilt over deceiving a blind woman, and the situation escalates to her voluntarily handing him the legal case against his family. The 'most feared prosecutor' has been completely defeated by sympathy while Michael failed to be dishonest enough. Multiple layers of irony converge in one moment.

jokes.text: "On the next Arrested Development" tag: Gob's seal business update — "The seal deal fell through. Three of the seals died on their way to Chad."
jokes.setup: The episode has established Gob's sea animal trading business
jokes.punchline: Three trained seals died on a trip to Chad — delivered as a casual business update
jokes.explanation: The seal deal's grim conclusion — three dead seals, one surviving in Chad — is treated as a minor business inconvenience. The specificity of 'Chad' as the destination is a nice absurdist detail. Dark in a very AD way.

jokes.text: "On the next" stinger: Gob encounters a maritime lawyer — "How am I ever gonna find a maritime lawyer?" / Michael's fake identity appears / "I trade trained seals for a living."
jokes.setup: Gob needs a maritime lawyer for his dead seals
jokes.punchline: Michael — who posed as maritime lawyer Chareth Cutestory — is somehow now being solicited by his own brother for his fake legal specialty
jokes.explanation: A perfect double callback that closes the episode's two main running threads. Gob needs a maritime lawyer for his dead seal business — and the only maritime lawyer in his life is his brother's fake identity. The tag's compressed structure (callback on callback) is quintessential AD. "Do you ever listen?" / "Do you ever stop talking?" recaptures their domestic argument energy as a button.

jokes.text: "On the next" stinger: Tobias breaks into Maggie's house to steal evidence — only to discover she's not blind
jokes.setup: The family has decided to exploit Michael's relationship with Maggie; Tobias is sent to steal evidence
jokes.punchline: Tobias breaks in and discovers Maggie is not actually blind — the episode's big dramatic twist confirmed in the tag
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Maggie is not blind is both a satisfying episodic twist and a retroactive reframing of every Maggie joke in the episode. Every 'I can smell you' line, every 'let me see that smile,' every cane moment is now revealed as performance. The rewatch value for this entire episode increases dramatically. The episode title 'Justice Is Blind' becomes a triple entendre: the legal concept, the dog's name, and the ironic truth that Justice (Maggie) is not blind at all.

tmdb_overview: Gob steals a file on the government's case against the Bluth Company that Maggie had given to Michael. Tobias is sent to Maggie's house to get evidence, George Michael tries to prove Maeby's fraud, and Lindsay fights to remove the Ten Commandments from the courthouse lawn.
insight: Wordplay and character comedy sustain an 80.8 score across fifty-eight rapid jokes.
